summary: Joel Spolsky contrasts the social experience of working at giant tech corporations like Google and Microsoft with that of a small company. He compares large company campuses to anonymous university cafeterias where thousands of coworkers are strangers, while his own small company's lunchtimes feel like intimate Thanksgiving dinners shared with people you genuinely know and like.
key_points: Giant tech corporations feel anonymous despite their impressive campuses and perks
Small companies offer a fundamentally different social experience that large companies cannot replicate
The quality of workplace relationships matters more than the scale of workplace amenities
money_quotes: Giant corporations such as Google and Microsoft are like cities full of relatively anonymous people: You don't actually expect to see anyone you know as you walk around.
Going to lunch on either campus is like going to the cafeteria at a huge university. The other 2,000 students seem nice, but you don't know most of them well enough to sit with them.
A typical lunchtime at my company is like Thanksgiving dinner: There's a big meal you get to share with a bunch of people you know and like.
key_insight: The intimate social bonds of a small company create a workplace experience that no amount of big-company perks and scale can replicate.
